coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen sulfur co doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probe for quantitative determination of monosodium glutamate in food samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Mansour, Fotouh R.
Hamid, Mohamed Ahmed Abdel
Gamal, Aya
Elagamy, Samar H.
coredata.dc:description: A validated, simple, and ecofriendly spectrofluorometric method has been developed for the determination of monosodium glutamate (MSG) in food matrices utilizing novel nitrogen and sulfur co doped carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) as fluorescent probes. MSG is one of the most commonly used food additives that has distinct taste. The proposed method is based on the finding that MSG can effectively enhance the fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs as a result of the formation of CQDS-MSG complex via electrostatic interaction and electron transfer from the excited CQDs to MSG. NS-CQDs have been synthesized using citric acid and D, L- methionine as precursors and the formed quantum dots were characterized by FTIR spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). NS-CQDs display bright blue fluorescence at emission wavelength 440 nm after excitation wavelength 330 nm with a quantum yield of 38.68% and a production yield of 88.75%. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs was found to be stable over a wide range of pH value (4.0–10). Under optimal experimental conditions, there was a linear relationship between the increased fluorescence and MSG concentration over the range of 25–250 µg/mL with limit of detection and limit of quantification 6.20 and 18.79 µg/mL respectively. NS-CQDs show excellent selectivity to MSG and the proposed method has been applied for MSG determination in two food products with satisfactory recoveries achieved by standard addition method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and sulfur co doped carbon quantum dots
Monosodium glutamate
Microwave method
Fluorescence enhancement
Flavors

Statistical comparison with a reference method
Green assessment of the developed method

a) TEM image of the prepared NS-CQDS and b) its size distribution histogram.
FTIR spectrum showing the functional groups of the prepared NS-CQDS.
The green analytical procedure index (GAPI) of the developed method.
The result of AGREE analysis of the developed method.

Evaluation of the accuracy for determination of MSG by the developed method.
Evaluation of the precision for determination of MSG by the developed method.
Evaluation of the robustness of the developed method.
Determination of MSG in the instant noodles and chicken cubes by standard addition method.
Matrix effect assessment in chicken cubes and instant noodles.
Assay of MSG in instant noodles and chicken cubes by the developed and reported methods.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The analytical eco scale of the developed method.
Comparison between the developed method and other reported methods for MSG determination in different food matrices.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen sulfur co doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probe for quantitative determination of monosodium glutamate in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, Tanta, Egypt, 31111

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al Salam University, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: The Medical Campus of Tanta University, Elgeish Street, Tanta 31111, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Medical Campus of Tanta University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A validated, simple, and ecofriendly spectrofluorometric method has been developed for the determination of monosodium glutamate (MSG) in food matrices utilizing novel nitrogen and sulfur co doped carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) as fluorescent probes. MSG is one of the most commonly used food additives that has distinct taste. The proposed method is based on the finding that MSG can effectively enhance the fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs as a result of the formation of CQDS-MSG complex via electrostatic interaction and electron transfer from the excited CQDs to MSG. NS-CQDs have been synthesized using citric acid and D, L- methionine as precursors and the formed quantum dots were characterized by FTIR spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). NS-CQDs display bright blue fluorescence at emission wavelength 440 nm after excitation wavelength 330 nm with a quantum yield of 38.68% and a production yield of 88.75%. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs was found to be stable over a wide range of pH value (4.0–10). Under optimal experimental conditions, there was a linear relationship between the increased fluorescence and MSG concentration over the range of 25–250 µg/mL with limit of detection and limit of quantification 6.20 and 18.79 µg/mL respectively. NS-CQDs show excellent selectivity to MSG and the proposed method has been applied for MSG determination in two food products with satisfactory recoveries achieved by standard addition method.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulfur co doped carbon quantum dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Monosodium glutamate (MSG) is the sodium salt of glutamic acid. It has been widely used in food products as a flavor enhancer and it plays an important role in different fields as amino acid biosynthesis, food industry and pharmaceutical dosage forms (Ali et al. 2021; Anirudhan and Alexander, 2015). MSG is associated with a Chinese restaurant syndrome that results from excessive ingestion of MSG with symptoms such as headache, flushing, nausea and chest pain (Al-Otaibi et al., 2022). The high consumption of MSG may result in neurological symptoms as Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s disease. Different analytical methods have been reported for determination of MSG as chromatographic techniques (Soyseven et al., 2021; Soyseven and Arli, 2021), spectrophotometric (Elattar et al., 2023), fluorometric methods (Kamal et al., 2023) and electrochemical detection (Devi et al., 2019).

Recently, carbon based nanomaterials as graphene and carbon quantum dots have been received growing attention in many applications owing to their unique properties such as cost effectiveness, ecofriendly, low toxicity, low preparation and water solubility (Chung et al., 2021; Du and Guo, 2016; Wang et al., 2019). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are typically composed of carbon cores and surface groups as OH, COOH, NH2, −SH, etc., which can be modified by doping atoms as N, S, P, B to increase the quantum yield and improve the fluorescence properties of the CQDs (Kandasamy, 2019) that may lead to many different applications such as bioimaging (Li et al., 2021; Tan et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021), biosensing (Wu et al., 2017), metal sensing (El-Malla et al., 2022; Kalaiyarasan et al., 2020; Liao et al., 2018; Preethi et al., 2022) and pharmaceutical analysis (Ahmed Abdel Hamid et al., 2023; El-Malla et al., 2021; Elshenawy et al., 2023).

Nitrogen proves to be an effective dopant owing to its substantial atomic size, strong electronegative character, availability of five valence electrons for chemical bonding, and possession of a lone pair of electrons that can readily integrate into the orbitals of sp2 carbon structures (Munusamy et al., 2023). In addition, Sulfur (S) is often used to improve the properties of optical carbon dots because of the similar electronegativity with carbon but a larger atomic radius than a carbon atom. Six valence electrons in each S atom can modify the electronic structure of CQDs (Kamali et al., 2021). Consequently, numerous studies have been dedicated to exploring the impact of nitrogen and sulfur doping and other modifications on the structure of CQDs (Elshenawy et al., 2022; Magdy et al., 2023).

There are many different methods to synthetize CQDs such as hydrothermal (John et al., 2023; Shen et al., 2021), microwave (Salman et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2018), solvothermal (Pontes et al., 2022), laser ablation (Cui et al., 2020) and carbonization (Li Liu et al., 2017). The microwave method has many advantages as simplicity, short reaction time, ecofriendly and reducing the energy consumption.

Herein, we present a new one-step microwave-assisted method for the synthesis of NS-CQDs, utilizing citric acid and D, L-methionine as starting materials. The proposed method is regarded as significantly simpler and faster in comparison to the reported hydrothermal methods (Ahmed et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Luo and Jiang, 2019). No previous fluorometric methods based on using CQDs have been reported for the determination of MSG. Thus, the aim of this study is to utilize the newly synthesized NS-CQDs as a fluorescent probe for the determination of MSG based on a turn-on mechanism.

MSG 99.00%, citric acid 99.00%, (D, L)-methionine 98.9% were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany. NaOH (99%), KI (99%) and Quinine sulfate were purchased from ISO-CHEM laboratory. H2SO4 and HCl were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Distilled water was utilized throughout the experiments. Instant noodles and chicken cubes were purchased from local markets.

A JASCO FP-6300 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) was used for the measurement of the fluorescence spectra. UV absorption spectra were measured using Shimadzu UV-1800 double beam spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tokyo, Japan) and Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) (Utah, United States) were employed for characterization of the synthesized NS-CQDs. The zeta potential analysis was carried out using ZetaPALS potential analyzer (Brookhaven, USA). A Tornado microwave oven (1000 watt, model MOM-C36BBE-S) and a benchtop centrifuge (Hettich, Germany) were utilized for the synthesis of NS-CQDs. The pH measurements were performed using pH-meter (HANNA, USA) with double junction glass electrode calibrated by standard buffers.

0.5 g citric acid and 0.3 g D, L-methionine were dissolved in distilled water in 10.0 mL beaker. Then the mixture was sonicated, transferred to crucible, and placed in microwave oven 800 watt for 60 s until the formation of yellowish layer. After that the formed NS-CQDs were cooled and 5.0 mL distilled water was added. A dialyzing membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 10 kDa was utilized to purify and homogenize CQDs, removing residual precursors and large molecule byproducts. The dialysis bag retained the residuals inside and allowed a clear solution of CQDs to be obtained outside. The fluorescence intensities were measured at emission wavelength 440 nm after excitation with 330 nm.

The quantum yield (Qu) was measured by using quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 as a reference with Qst equal 0.54. Qu was calculated according to the following equation.
Q u = Q st (
F u
Fst
) (
Ast
Au
) (
η u
η st
) 2
Where F refers to the fluorescence intensity after excitation at 330 nm, A represents the absorbance values at 330 nm and η refers to the solvents refractive index that equals 1.33 for both solvents.

The production yield (PY) of the developed NS-CQDs was measured as described by Zheng et al. (Zheng et al., 2020). The solid NS-CQDs powder was acquired through the lyophilization of the NS-CQDs solution, then the PY was calculated using the following equation:
PY =
m
CQDs
m
s
× 100
Where ms is the weight of starting materials (0.8 g), and mCQDs is the weight of the NS-CQDs powder (0.71 g). The production yield was found to be 88.75% and the final concentration of the NS-CQDs after dilution in 100 mL of distilled water was calculated to be 7.1 mg/mL.

Stock solution of MSG was prepared by dissolving 0.01 g in 10 mL distilled water (1.0 mg/mL). In 10 mL flask, different volumes of MSG (250–2500 µL) were added to 500 µL NS-CQDs to obtain final concentration of MSG (25–250 µg/mL). The pH was adjusted to 7 using 0.1 mol/L sodium hydroxide. The fluorescence intensities were measured instantly at 440 nm after excitation at 330 nm. The blank was prepared in 10 mL flask by adding 500 µL NS-CQDs and completed to the volume by distilled water. The calibration curve was constructed by plotting the concentration of MSG against the fluorescence ratio F/F0 where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs in the presence and absence of MSG, respectively as shown in Fig. S1.

The noodles contain powder sachet that weighs 4.677 g. In 100 mL flask, 0.502 g of this powder is weighed and dissolved with distilled water. After that, the solution was sonicated and filtered to prepare the noodles stock solution. In 10 mL flask, 500 µL of stock solution was added to 500 µL NS-CQDs and adjusted to the volume with distilled water. The fluorescence intensities were measured at 440 nm after excitation at 330 nm and the amount of MSG present in the sample is computed from the calibration curve (105.9 µg/mL). Standard addition method was performed for accurate quantitative analysis through addition of different volumes (500, 1000, 1200 µL) of MSG standard solution 1.0 mg/mL to 500 µL of stock solution. Then, the resulting solutions were treated with 500 µL NS-CQDs and their corresponding fluorescence intensities were measured.

A 0.5 g of chicken cubes was crushed using a mortar and pestle then transferred to 100 mL flask and dissolved in distilled water. The formed solution was sonicated and filtered to form the stock solution. In 10 mL flask, 500 µL of the stock solution was added to 500 µL NS-CQDs and completed the volume with distilled water. The fluorescence intensities were measured and the amount of MSG present in the sample is calculated from the calibration curve (81.5 µg/mL). Similar to the assay of instant noodles, standard addition method was carried out through addition of different volumes (1000, 1200, 1400 µL) of MSG standard solution 1.0 mg/mL to 500 µL of stock solution. Then, the resulting solutions were treated with 500 µL NS-CQDs and their corresponding fluorescence intensities were measured.

The synthesis of highly fluorescent NS-CQDs using microwave assisted method was achieved in one minute using cheap and available starting materials with citric acid as carbon source and D,L-methionine as nitrogen and sulfur dopant Fig. 1. The quantum yield was found to be 38%. NS-CQDs exhibit blue fluorescence when exposed to the UV light and the fluorescence intensity was constant for 30 min. The morphology features and particle size distribution of the prepared NS-CQDs were examined using TEM. TEM images reveal that they are well distributed with spherical shapes and separated from each other without obvious aggregation with average particle size of 6.0 nm Fig. 2. FTIR spectroscopic analysis was utilized to determine the chemical structure properties of NS-CQDs. The IR spectrum shows the following stretching frequencies: O-H/N-H at (3500–3100 cm−1), S-H at (2395 cm−1), CC at (1450 cm−1), CO at (1750 cm−1) and CS at (1300 cm−1) Fig. 3. In addition, elemental analysis using elemental analyzer CHNS was used for further characterization of NS-CQDs and the results show that the composition of NS-CQDs are C (25.25%), N (8.3%), S (5%). These results indicate that the prepared CQDs contain carbon core with highly water solubility with limited amount of sulfur that comes from the sulfhydryl group of methionine.

The fluorescence spectrum of NS-CQDs was measured under different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 390 nm Fig. S2. The emission intensity reached the maximum at 440 nm after excitation at 330 nm. The most likely explanation of this excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior is the existence of the different surface organic groups on the NS-CQDs (Gan et al., 2016).

There was also significant increase in the fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs after addition of MSG Fig. S3. This fluorescence enhancement is attributed to the electrostatic interaction and electron transfer between the carboxylic groups on the surface of NS-CQDs and the amine group of MSG (Zeng et al., 2017).

The initial investigation was conducted using a solution containing 150 µg/mL of MSG and 500 µL of NS-CQDs at pH 7. The optimization study focused on analyzing all the factors influencing the interaction between the NS-CQDs and MSG, with a systematic examination of each factor individually while keeping the other factors constant.

The effect of pH was optimized in the range of pH (1.0–10) using sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid, covering acidic to alkaline conditions. The pH has a critical role as it does not only affect the fluorescence intensities but also the interactions between NS-CQDs and MSG. It was found that, the highly acidic media from pH 1.0 to 3.0 decrease the fluorescence intensity. This effect can be explained by the protonation of the carboxylic and amino groups on the surface of the NS-CQDs and formation of hydrogen bond that lead to aggregation of the NS-CQDs resulting in lowering the fluorescence intensity. The pH 7 was selected for maximum fluorescence stability as shown in Fig. S4. The pH trend was verified through zeta potential analysis, revealing a value of − 4.9 mV at pH 3 and − 12.3 mV at pH 7. This indicates that the negative charge on the surface on NS-CQDs has increased by increasing the pH and this explains the electrostatic interaction between the amine group of MSG and the carboxylic group of NS-CQDs.

The incubation time after the addition of MSG to NS-CQDs was also optimized. The effect of incubation time was studied by measuring the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm as a function of time. This determination was carried out over a 30-minute period with measurements recorded at 1-minute intervals. As shown in Fig. S5, the interaction between NS-CQDs and MSG was instantaneous, without the need for any waiting periods.

Different volumes of potassium iodide KI (1.0 mg/mL) were added to investigate the effect of ionic strength on NS-CQDs as shown Fig. S6. It was found that, the ionic strength has no effect on the fluorescence intensities which indicates that there is no ionization groups on the surface of NS-CQDs. This negligible effect of the ionic strength could be due to high affinity and the strong electrostatic interaction between CQDs and functional groups of MSG.

The effect of NS-CQDs volume was investigated in the range of 200–1000 µL with the aim of achieving the highest F/F0 value. The results indicated a consistent F/F0 within the NS-CQDs volume range of 400–600 µL. As a result, the optimal volume of 500 µL was selected, as illustrated in Fig. S7.
The proposed method was validated according to ICH guidelines (Dagron, 2014) in terms of linearity, range, limit of detection and quantification, accuracy, precision and robustness.
The fluorescence intensities linearly increased with increasing the concentration of MSG over the range 25–250 µg/mL Fig. S8. The regression parameters were listed in Table (1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ)
ce:section.ce:para:
The LOD and LOQ were calculated from the following equations where σ refers to the standard deviation of the intercept and s refers to the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD and LOQ were 6.02 µg/mL and 18.79 µg/mL respectively.
LOD=3·3 σ/S LOQ=10 σ/S

The accuracy of the analytical method is the closeness the results to the true value. To test the accuracy of the proposed method, three different concentrations of MSG within the linearity range were determined in triplicate. The accuracy was determined by calculating the % recovery. According to Table (2), the % recovery of the proposed method fall within the accepted range 98–102%.

The precision of the proposed method was measured by evaluating the intraday and interday precision. The intraday precision was assessed by determination of three replicates of three different concentrations of MSG within the linearity range in the same day. Similar procedures were repeated for estimation the interday precision in three successive days. The results were summarized in Table 3. The results obtained show that the %RSD less than 2% which indicate that the method is precise.

The robustness of the prepared method was evaluated by studying the effect of small deliberate changes in the parameters of the fluorescence response. The studied parameters were pH (7 ± 0.2), incubation time (1.0 min interval), emission wavelength (440 ± 2.0 nm) and excitation wavelength (330 ± 2.0 nm). It was found that these minor changes have no effect on the performance of the method that indicates the robustness of the method Table 4.

The developed method was successfully applied for the determination of MSG in chicken stock cubes and instant noodles. Standard addition method was carried out to minimize the matrix effect and to determine if there were any interactions between the analyte and matrix. The %recoveries were (95–105%) with %RSD less than 2%. The results were listed in Table 5.

The matrix effect was assessed in this method by the construction of calibration curves in chicken cubes and instant noddle. The slopes of the matrix-based calibration curves were calculated and compared with the solvent-based calibration curves. The results indicated negligible differences between them, as shown in Table 6, with relative standard deviation values of the slopes consistently below 15%. Therefore, the developed method demonstrated both selectivity and applicability across different matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical comparison with a reference method
ce:section.ce:para: The instant noodles and chicken stock cubes were also assayed using a reported spectrofluorometric method (Kamal et al., 2023). The results obtained from the developed and reported methods are statistically compared. Student t-test and F-test were calculated and found that there is no significantly difference between the two methods as shown in Table 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green assessment of the developed method
ce:section.ce:para: The greenness profile of the proposed method was assessed using three tools by the analytical eco scale (Gałuszka et al., 2012), the green analytical procedure index (GAPI) (Płotka-Wasylka, 2018) and Analytical Greenness metric approach (AGREE) (Pena-Pereira et al., 2020). The score of the first tool, analytical eco-scale is calculated by subtracting total penalty points from a basic value of 100. The penalty points are assigned to the amounts of the solvents and reagents employed, energy consumption, occupational hazards, and amount of waste. As shown in Table 8, the proposed method gives a score of 94, which proves that the method is excellent green. The second tool, GAPI comprises a pictogram with a color pattern (green, yellow, and red) to classify the environmental impact of each step of the analytical method. Fig. 4 shows the GAPI pictogram of the proposed method. Table S1 shows detailed explanation of GAPI results. The input for the third tool, AGREE calculator is 12 principles of green analytical chemistry which is then converted into a standard scale of 0–1. The result of the calculation is pictogram with twelve sections with the final assessment score in the center. Each section has a specific color range from dark green (=1) to dark red (=0) Table S2. Fig. 5 shows the results of AGREE metrics for the proposed method.

The determination of MSG is a problematic due to lack of fluorophore. Most previously reported techniques have relied on sophisticated techniques and tedious sample preparation procedures. Herein, we compare our developed method with the other reported method utilizing nanoparticles for determination of MSG in different food samples (Atilgan et al., 2023; Devi et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021; Sharma et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023). The results are summarized in Table 9. Despite the highly sensitivity of these methods, they predominantly employed electrochemical detection, which is susceptible to temperature variations and has a limited shelf life. Moreover, the approach for synthesis of nanoparticles in these method is quite complicated. Thus, the developed method offers distinct advantages in terms of simplicity, time efficiency, environmental friendliness, and the absence of specialized equipment requirements. The simplicity of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesis and associated procedures render this method a preferable and more convenient choice compared to previous methods.

In this study, NS-CQDs were synthesized through a novel approach based on microwave assisted method in only 60 s using citric acid and D, L-methionine. The synthesized NS-CQDs have strong blue emission at 440 nm after excitation at 330 nm with a quantum yield of 38.68% and a production yield of 88.75%. The fluorescence intensities of NS-CQDs were enhanced linearly after the addition of MSG in the range of 25–250 µg/mL due to the formation of CQDS-MSG complex. Therefore, they were utilized as fluorescent probe for quantitative determination of MSG in food samples. All factors affecting the fluorescence stability and intensity were optimized. The method has been applied for MSG determination in instant noodles and chicken cubes and satisfactory results were obtained with %recoveries in the range of (95–105%). Based on the results, it can be concluded that the proposed method is simple, fast, selective, and can be readily used in practice.

The authors of the manuscript “Nitrogen sulfur co doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probe for quantitative determination of monosodium glutamate in food samples” declare that there are no conflicts of interest among the authors related to this research.

Mansour Fotouh R.: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Abdel Hamid Mohamed Ahmed: Supervision, Investigation. Gamal Aya: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Data curation. Elagamy Samar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors declare that there are no financial or non-financial hat are directly or indirectly related to this manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2024.105972.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
